name: Marrakech
description:
Marrakech, known as the Red City for its distinctive terracotta-colored buildings and walls, is one of Morocco's most enchanting destinations. Founded in 1070, this former imperial city sits at the foot of the Atlas Mountains and serves as a gateway between the Sahara Desert and the Atlantic coast.

The heart of Marrakech is the Medina, a UNESCO World Heritage Site enclosed by 19 kilometers of ancient walls. Within this labyrinth of narrow alleyways (derbs), you'll discover traditional riads (courtyard houses), mosques, madrasas, and bustling souks. The Ben Youssef Madrasa, a 14th-century Islamic college, features some of the finest examples of Moorish architecture in North Africa, with intricate geometric tilework, carved cedar wood, and stucco ornamentation.

Jemaa el-Fnaa, the main square, transforms throughout the day from a relatively quiet morning market to an extraordinary open-air spectacle by evening. Snake charmers, storytellers, acrobats, and musicians compete for attention, while dozens of food stalls serve traditional dishes amid clouds of aromatic smoke. The surrounding souks are organized by trade — you'll find entire streets dedicated to leather goods, metalwork, textiles, spices, and carpets.

Moroccan cuisine is a feast for the senses. Tagines — slow-cooked stews prepared in conical clay pots — come in countless varieties, from chicken with preserved lemons and olives to lamb with prunes and almonds. Couscous, typically served on Fridays, is hand-rolled and steamed to fluffy perfection. Street food includes msemen (layered flatbread), harira (tomato and lentil soup), and freshly squeezed orange juice from vendors in the square. Mint tea, poured from a height to create a frothy top, is offered as a gesture of hospitality everywhere you go.

The Majorelle Garden, created by French painter Jacques Majorelle and later owned by Yves Saint Laurent, is a stunning botanical garden featuring cobalt blue buildings, exotic plants from five continents, and tranquil fountains.
best_time: March to May, September to November
fun_fact: The souks of Marrakech contain over 3,000 shops, and skilled navigators say it takes years to learn all the routes through the Medina.
